keyIdeas: To enable learners to identify and describe the specific components that form the earth loop impedance path (Zs) in an electrical installation.

Ze (External Earth Loop Impedance) and the supplier earth return path.

R1 (Line conductor), R2 (CPC), and the MET (Main Earthing Terminal).

The formula Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2) as the total earth loop impedance.
The total impedance (Zs) determines if the circuit is safe and meets BS 7671 requirements.

steps.title: The Concept of the Loop
steps.objective: Define the earth loop impedance path as a complete circuit.
keyIdeas: Fault current requires a complete loop to travel back to the transformer.
The total resistance of this loop (Zs) determines if protective devices will operate correctly.
The fault current path from the source, through the installation, and back to the source.
Fault current must have a low-resistance path to ensure protective devices operate correctly.
Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.
anchorFacts: Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.
The Earth Loop Impedance Path is the complete journey that fault current takes from the transformer, through the installation, and back again.
We use the symbol Zs to represent the total resistance (impedance) of this entire loop.
A low Zs value is essential because it ensures enough current flows during a fault to trip a circuit breaker or blow a fuse instantly.
Fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: Earth is a 'sink' where electricity just disappears. | Correct belief: Electricity must return to its source (the transformer) via a complete loop.

Electricity 'disappears' into the earth or an earth rod without returning to the source. | Think of the circuit as a continuous circle that starts and ends at the supply transformer. | Consider what is required for any electrical current to flow continuously.

steps.title: External Path Components
steps.objective: Identify the components of the path external to the installation.
keyIdeas: Ze is the foundation of the loop provided by the network distributor.
The external path includes everything from the transformer to the building's main intake.
Ze (External Earth Loop Impedance) and the supplier earth return path.
The external impedance is the foundation of the total loop and is provided by the network distributor.
The part of the loop that exists outside of the building is called the external earth loop impedance, abbreviated as Ze.
Ze includes the resistance of the transformer windings at the substation and the supplier's distribution cables.
anchorFacts: The part of the loop that exists outside of the building is called the external earth loop impedance, abbreviated as Ze.
Ze includes the resistance of the transformer windings at the substation and the supplier's distribution cables.
The supplier earth return path is the physical route (such as the cable sheath or the ground) provided by the network distributor for fault current.
Electricians measure Ze at the origin of the installation, usually at the main intake, to find the 'starting' resistance of the system.
Because Ze is provided by the supplier, the electrician has no direct control over its value, but must account for it in safety calculations.
Ze is the external impedance measured at the origin of the installation.
Supplier Earth Return Path is the name of the physical route provided by the network distributor for fault current to return to the source.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: The electrician has direct control over the resistance of the supplier's cables. | Correct belief: Ze is the external part of the loop measured at the origin of the installation.
The electrician can lower the Ze value by changing internal wiring. | The 'e' in Ze stands for 'External'. | Think about how the external resistance affects the final total resistance of the circuit.

steps.title: Internal Path Components
steps.objective: Identify the components of the path within the installation.
keyIdeas: R1 is the resistance of the line conductor; R2 is the resistance of the circuit protective conductor.
The MET is the 'hub' where the internal house earthing meets the supplier's external earth.
R1 (Line conductor), R2 (CPC), and the MET (Main Earthing Terminal).
These are the components installed by the electrician that contribute to the total circuit impedance.
Inside the building, the fault current travels through the line conductor to the point of the fault; we label this resistance R1.
After the fault occurs, the current returns through the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which we label as R2.
anchorFacts: Inside the building, the fault current travels through the line conductor to the point of the fault; we label this resistance R1.
After the fault occurs, the current returns through the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which we label as R2.
The Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the central hub where all internal earthing and bonding conductors connect.
The MET acts as the bridge where the internal path (R1 and R2) meets the external path (Ze).
R1 and R2 are specific to each individual circuit and are determined by the length and size of the cables installed by the electrician.
R1 is the resistance of the line conductor.
MET is the name of the central hub where internal earthing conductors connect to the supplier's earth.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: R1 and R2 are the same thing. | Correct belief: R1 is the resistance of the line conductor; R2 is the resistance of the circuit protective conductor.

R1 and R2 are the same thing; they actually represent two different legs of the internal circuit. | R1 is the 'outbound' wire to the fault, and R2 is the 'return' earth wire. | Focus on the MET's role as a meeting point for different parts of the loop.

steps.title: The Total Path (Zs)
steps.objective: Explain the relationship between all components in the path.
keyIdeas: Zs is the total resistance of the entire journey from the source and back.
The relationship between Ze, R1, and R2 determines the overall safety of the circuit.
The formula Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2) as the total earth loop impedance.
The total impedance (Zs) determines if the circuit is safe and meets BS 7671 requirements.
The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external path and the internal path.
The standard formula used by electricians is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
anchorFacts: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external path and the internal path.
The standard formula used by electricians is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
By adding the external resistance (Ze) to the internal cable resistances (R1 + R2), we find the total resistance a fault current will face.
This total value must be low enough to allow a high 'fault current' to flow, which forces the fuse or breaker to disconnect the power within the time limits set by BS 7671.
If the Zs value is too high, the circuit is considered unsafe because the protective device might not trip during a fault.
The formula for total earth loop impedance is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: You only need to know the CPC resistance to find the fault path. | Correct belief: The path includes the line conductor, the CPC, the MET, and the external supplier path.

You only need to know the CPC resistance (R2) to understand the fault path. | To get the total (Zs), you must add the external part (Ze) to the internal parts (R1 and R2). | Consider the relationship between resistance, current, and the speed of the protective device.

steps.title: Worked Example: Tracing the Fault Path
steps.objective: Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).
keyIdeas: Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).
Show one worked example.
Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.

Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.

Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.

Step 4: Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.
steps: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.

Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal components: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2). All parts must be intact for the circuit breaker to trip safely.

steps.hint: Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier.

steps.objective: Explanation B
keyIdeas: Ze is the external earth loop impedance provided by the network distributor.
It includes the resistance of the transformer windings and the supplier's distribution cables.
The Supplier Earth Return Path is the physical route (like cable armouring) provided for fault current.
anchorFacts: Ze is measured at the origin of the installation (the main intake).
The electrician has no direct control over the Ze value as it is part of the supplier's network.
Ze forms the foundation of the total loop impedance.
Supplier Earth Return Path is the name of the physical route provided by the network distributor for fault current to return to the source.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: The electrician can lower the Ze value by changing internal wiring. | Correct belief: Ze is external; internal changes only affect R1 and R2.
taskConstraints: Focus on the 'external' nature of Ze.
steps.questionIntent: Identify the external components of the loop.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Understand Ze as the foundation for safety calculations.

basicQuestions.questionText: What abbreviation represents the external earth loop impedance provided by the supplier?
At what specific point in an electrical installation is the external impedance measured?
What is the name of the physical route provided by the network distributor for fault current to return to the source?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main reason an electrician measure Ze at the origin if they cannot change the supplier's equipment?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ze is the starting resistance for the whole loop
It is needed to calculate the total Zs
Ensures the supplier's path is adequate before adding internal wiring
steps.hint: The 'e' in Ze stands for 'External'.

steps.title: Internal Path Components
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: components of an earth loop impedance path.
keyIdeas: R1 is the resistance of the line conductor from the consumer unit to the fault.
R2 is the resistance of the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) from the fault back to the consumer unit.
The Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the central hub where internal earthing meets the supplier's earth.
anchorFacts: R1 and R2 are determined by the length and size of the cables installed by the electrician.
The MET acts as the bridge between the internal path and the external path.
All internal earthing and bonding conductors connect to the MET.
MET is the name of the central hub where internal earthing conductors connect to the supplier's earth.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: R1 and R2 are the same thing. | Correct belief: R1 is the 'outbound' line conductor; R2 is the 'return' earth conductor.
taskConstraints: Clearly distinguish between R1 and R2.
steps.questionIntent: Identify the internal components of the loop.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the MET's role as a bridge.

basicQuestions.questionText: What label is used for the resistance of the line conductor within an installation?
What does the abbreviation R2 represent in the context of an earth loop path?
What is the name of the central hub where internal earthing conductors connect to the supplier's earth?

steps.deeperQuestionText: What is the main way the MET act as a critical junction between the electrician's work and the network distributor's equipment?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It bridges the internal R1/R2 path and the external Ze path
Connection point for all earthing and bonding
Allows internal fault current to reach the supplier return path
steps.hint: R1 is the 'outbound' wire to the fault, and R2 is the 'return' earth wire.

steps.title: The Total Path (Zs)
steps.objective: Teach the core idea: 203.LO4.AC4.5.
keyIdeas: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal paths.
The standard formula is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
Zs must be low enough to allow sufficient fault current to trip the protective device within BS 7671 time limits.
anchorFacts: High Zs values result in low fault current, which may prevent a breaker from tripping.
BS 7671 defines the maximum allowable disconnection times based on Zs.
The formula Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2) combines all components of the path.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: You only need to know the CPC resistance to find the fault path. | Correct belief: The path includes the line conductor, CPC, MET, and the external supplier path.
taskConstraints: Focus on the relationship between Zs and safety (disconnection time).
steps.questionIntent: Verify understanding of the Zs formula and its safety implications.
steps.deeperQuestionIntent: Explain the link between high resistance and slow disconnection.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the standard formula used to determine the total earth loop impedance?
If the Zs value of a circuit is too high, what happens to the level of fault current that can flow?
Which set of regulations defines the maximum allowable disconnection times for a circuit?

steps.deeperQuestionText: Why would a circuit with a very high Zs value be considered a fire or shock risk even if the fuse is the correct size?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Fault current will be too low to trip the device instantly
Protective device may not disconnect within BS 7671 time limits
Current continues to flow through the fault
High resistance prevents rapid operation of the fuse or breaker
steps.hint: To get the total (Zs), you must add the external part (Ze) to the internal parts (R1 and R2).

steps.title: Worked Example: Tracing the Fault Path
steps.objective: Show one worked example.
keyIdeas: Trace the path of a fault current from a socket outlet back to the supply transformer.
Label each component encountered in the journey.
anchorFacts: Step 1: Outbound path (R1) - Current travels from the consumer unit to the socket via the line conductor.
Step 2: Internal return path (R2) - Current enters the CPC at the fault and travels back to the consumer unit.
Step 3: Connection point (MET) - The CPC connects to the MET, bridging internal and external paths.
Step 4: External path (Ze) - Current leaves the building via the Supplier Earth Return Path to the transformer.
taskConstraints: Maintain a logical sequence from fault to source.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.
steps: Identify R1: The resistance of the line conductor from the consumer unit to the socket.
Identify R2: The resistance of the CPC from the socket back to the consumer unit.
Identify MET: The terminal where the CPC meets the supplier's earthing conductor.
Identify Ze: The external resistance from the MET back to the supply transformer.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: The total path (Zs) is the sum of all these parts: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).

steps.hint: Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier.

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Guide the learner through a supported task.
keyIdeas: Identify the internal outbound component of the earth loop impedance path.
anchorFacts: The line conductor carries current to the point of the fault.
Its resistance is labeled R1.
taskConstraints: Focus on a single component identification.
steps.questionIntent: Identify R1 in a practical scenario.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A fault occurs at a metal electric heater. The fault current starts its journey at the consumer unit and travels to the heater through the brown line conductor.
steps: Identify the technical abbreviation for the resistance of this specific 'outgoing' leg of the internal circuit.

steps.questionText: What is the technical abbreviation for the resistance of this line conductor within the earth loop path?
steps.hint: Think of the internal components: R1 is the line conductor and R2 is the CPC.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Understanding Check A
keyIdeas: Identify the total earth loop impedance abbreviation in a certification context.
anchorFacts: Zs is the value recorded on certificates representing the total loop resistance.
taskConstraints: Focus on the 'total' nature of Zs.
steps.questionIntent: Identify Zs as the total value.

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are completing an Electrical Installation Certificate. You have measured the external resistance (Ze) and added it to the combined resistance of the line conductor and CPC (R1 + R2).
steps: Identify the specific technical abbreviation for this total combined resistance.

steps.questionText: What is the specific technical abbreviation for the total earth loop impedance?
steps.hint: This is the 'Total' value calculated by the formula: Ze + (R1 + R2).

steps.title: The Complete Fault Journey
steps.objective: Integrative
keyIdeas: Synthesize the external and internal components into a single journey.
Trace the path from the fault back to the source.
anchorFacts: The path must be continuous from the fault back to the transformer to ensure safety.
taskConstraints: Use the correct sequence of components.
Avoid the 'sink' misconception by ending at the transformer.
steps.questionIntent: Sequence the components of the return path correctly.

taskSkeleton.scenario: A fault occurs at an appliance. To ensure the circuit breaker trips, the fault current must travel from the appliance's metal casing back to the source.
steps: List the components in order that the current passes through to return to the transformer.

steps.questionText: State the main fault path in order from the appliance back to Earth.
answerGuidance: CPC to MET to Earthing Conductor to Supplier Earth Return Path
steps.hint: Trace the current from the appliance's earth wire, through the main terminal, and out to the supplier's equipment.

issues.excerpt: ["CPC to MET to Earthing Conductor to Supplier Earth Return Path", "fault current path", "protective device trips"]
issues.problem: The answer guidance for the integrative sequence contains conceptual labels ('fault current path') and outcomes ('protective device trips') rather than the specific sequence items requested.
issues.whyItMatters: Automated marking systems may incorrectly validate a learner's response if the guidance includes non-target phrases or meta-descriptions of the task.
issues.suggestion: Remove 'fault current path' and 'protective device trips' from the answerGuidance array, leaving only the technical sequence.

issues.excerpt: "Earthing Conductor"
issues.problem: The term 'Earthing Conductor' is required in the final integrative sequence but was not explicitly defined or highlighted in the preceding teaching blocks.
issues.whyItMatters: Beginners may confuse the 'Earthing Conductor' with the 'CPC' or 'Supplier Earth Return Path' if the specific distinction (the link between MET and the supplier's terminal) isn't taught first.
issues.suggestion: Add a brief mention of the Earthing Conductor in Step 4 (Internal Path Components) as the physical link between the MET and the supplier's equipment.

issues.excerpt: What is the main reason an electrician measure Ze at the origin if they cannot change the supplier's equipment?
issues.problem: Grammatical error: 'an electrician measure' should be 'an electrician measures'.
issues.whyItMatters: Minor grammatical slips reduce the perceived authority of the 'strong electrical tutor' persona.
issues.suggestion: Correct to: 'What is the main reason an electrician measures Ze...'

Topic focus: Earth Loop Impedance Path

Lesson split rationale: Dedicated lesson for understanding the complex path of earth fault current and its constituent components.

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.

terms.simpleDefinition: The total resistance of the entire earth loop path, including both the supplier's equipment and the internal installation wiring.
terms.anchor: The 'Total' impedance that determines if a fuse will blow fast enough during a fault.
terms.misconception: Thinking Zs only refers to the earth wire; it actually includes the line conductor and the external supply path too.

terms.simpleDefinition: The resistance of the fault path outside of the installation, provided by the electricity supplier.
terms.anchor: The 'External' impedance measured at the origin of the installation.
terms.misconception: Believing the electrician can lower this value; Ze is determined by the supplier's cables and transformer.

terms.simpleDefinition: The resistance of the line (phase) conductor from the consumer unit to the point of the fault.
terms.anchor: The 'Outgoing' leg of the fault current journey.
terms.misconception: Confusing R1 with the total circuit resistance; it only accounts for the line wire.

terms.simpleDefinition: The resistance of the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) from the point of the fault back to the main earthing terminal.
terms.anchor: The 'Return' leg of the fault current within the building.
terms.misconception: Thinking R2 is the same as the main earthing conductor; R2 is specific to the individual circuit's earth wire.

terms.simpleDefinition: The Main Earthing Terminal; the central point where all earthing and bonding conductors are connected.
terms.anchor: The 'Hub' where the internal house earthing meets the supplier's external earth.
terms.misconception: Thinking the MET is just a junction box; it is a critical safety connection point for the entire installation.

terms.simpleDefinition: The physical route (cables or the ground itself) that fault current takes to get from the installation back to the supply transformer.
terms.anchor: The 'Road Home' for electricity to complete its circuit back to the source.
terms.misconception: Believing electricity just 'stops' at the earth rod or ground; it must always return to the transformer to complete the loop.

anchorPhrases: The fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.
Ze is the foundation of the loop provided by the network distributor.
R1 and R2 represent the internal resistance of the specific circuit.
misconceptionTargets: Electricity does not disappear into the ground; it returns to the transformer.
The earth loop is a circuit, not a one-way street.
You cannot calculate Zs accurately without including the line conductor (R1).

        "The Earth Loop Impedance Path is the complete journey that fault current takes from the transformer, through the installation, and back again.",
        "We use the symbol Zs to represent the total resistance (impedance) of this entire loop.",

      ],

    },

teachChecks.title: The Concept of the Loop
teachChecks.objective: Define the earth loop impedance path as a complete circuit.
teachingPoints: Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.
The Earth Loop Impedance Path is the complete journey that fault current takes from the transformer, through the installation, and back again.
We use the symbol Zs to represent the total resistance (impedance) of this entire loop.
A low Zs value is essential because it ensures enough current flows during a fault to trip a circuit breaker or blow a fuse instantly.
keyIdeas: Fault current requires a complete loop to travel back to the transformer.
The total resistance of this loop (Zs) determines if protective devices will operate correctly.
misconceptions: Electricity 'disappears' into the earth or an earth rod without returning to the source.
retrievalTextLines: Fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.
The symbol Zs stands for the total earth loop impedance of the circuit.
teachChecks.hint: Think of the circuit as a continuous circle that starts and ends at the supply transformer.

teachChecks.title: External Path Components
teachChecks.objective: Identify the components of the path external to the installation.
teachingPoints: The part of the loop that exists outside of the building is called the external earth loop impedance, abbreviated as Ze.
Ze includes the resistance of the transformer windings at the substation and the supplier's distribution cables.
The supplier earth return path is the physical route (such as the cable sheath or the ground) provided by the network distributor for fault current.
Electricians measure Ze at the origin of the installation, usually at the main intake, to find the 'starting' resistance of the system.
Because Ze is provided by the supplier, the electrician has no direct control over its value, but must account for it in safety calculations.
keyIdeas: Ze is the foundation of the loop provided by the network distributor.
The external path includes everything from the transformer to the building's main intake.
misconceptions: The electrician can lower the Ze value by changing internal wiring.
retrievalTextLines: Ze is the external impedance measured at the origin of the installation.
The supplier earth return path is the route provided by the network back to the transformer.

teachChecks.title: Internal Path Components
teachChecks.objective: Identify the components of the path within the installation.
teachingPoints: Inside the building, the fault current travels through the line conductor to the point of the fault; we label this resistance R1.
After the fault occurs, the current returns through the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which we label as R2.
The Main Earthing Terminal (MET) is the central hub where all internal earthing and bonding conductors connect.
The MET acts as the bridge where the internal path (R1 and R2) meets the external path (Ze).
R1 and R2 are specific to each individual circuit and are determined by the length and size of the cables installed by the electrician.
keyIdeas: R1 is the resistance of the line conductor; R2 is the resistance of the circuit protective conductor.
The MET is the 'hub' where the internal house earthing meets the supplier's external earth.
misconceptions: R1 and R2 are the same thing; they actually represent two different legs of the internal circuit.
retrievalTextLines: R1 is the resistance of the line conductor.
R2 is the resistance of the circuit protective conductor (CPC).
The MET is the Main Earthing Terminal.
teachChecks.hint: R1 is the 'outbound' wire to the fault, and R2 is the 'return' earth wire.

teachChecks.title: The Total Path (Zs)
teachChecks.objective: Explain the relationship between all components in the path.
teachingPoints: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external path and the internal path.
The standard formula used by electricians is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
By adding the external resistance (Ze) to the internal cable resistances (R1 + R2), we find the total resistance a fault current will face.
This total value must be low enough to allow a high 'fault current' to flow, which forces the fuse or breaker to disconnect the power within the time limits set by BS 7671.
If the Zs value is too high, the circuit is considered unsafe because the protective device might not trip during a fault.
keyIdeas: Zs is the total resistance of the entire journey from the source and back.
The relationship between Ze, R1, and R2 determines the overall safety of the circuit.
misconceptions: You only need to know the CPC resistance (R2) to understand the fault path.
retrievalTextLines: The formula for total earth loop impedance is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
Zs represents the total resistance of the entire fault current journey.
teachChecks.hint: To get the total (Zs), you must add the external part (Ze) to the internal parts (R1 and R2).

Grounded source text:
Unit: 203 - Electrical installations technology

Learning outcome: LO4 - Requirements for earthing systems

Lesson: 203-4C - Earth Loop Impedance Path

Topic focus: Earth Loop Impedance Path

Prerequisites: 203-4B

Must-have topics: components of an earth loop impedance path

Assessment criteria in scope:
- 203.LO4.AC4.5: Identify components of an earth loop impedance path

Lesson split rationale: Dedicated lesson for understanding the complex path of earth fault current and its constituent components.

In-scope teaching anchors: components of an earth loop impedance path

Out-of-scope topics: Earthing Systems and ADS Components; Exposed and Extraneous Conductive Parts

Known misconceptions to handle:
- Wrong belief: Any related term means the same thing in every context. | Correct belief: Terms in this lesson are distinct and must be used precisely.
- Wrong belief: You can answer checks without using taught explanation content. | Correct belief: Checks must be answered from concepts taught in the explanation blocks.

Grounded source excerpts:

Excerpt 1 (d9a6caef-6a5f-4977-9780-209ce55a5692): ## LO4 — Requirements for earthing systems **AC** 4.1 Identify earthing system types 4.2 Identify ADS component parts 4.3 Identify exposed conductive parts 4.4 Identify extraneous conductive parts 4.5 Identify components of an earth loop impedance path **Range** - Earthing systems: TT; TN-S; TN-C-S - ADS components: CPC; main protective bonding; supplementary bonding; earthing conductor; protective devices; earth electrode - Exposed conductive parts: steel conduit/trunking/tray; steel enclosures; metal accessories; metallic equipment - Extraneous conductive parts: metallic gas/oil/water pipes; steel ductwork; structural steel - Earth loop impedance path components: Zs; Ze; R1; R2; MET; supplier earth return path ---

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Zs",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total resistance of the entire earth loop path, including both the supplier's equipment and the internal installation wiring.",
      "anchor": "The 'Total' impedance that determines if a fuse will blow fast enough during a fault.",
      "misconception": "Thinking Zs only refers to the earth wire; it actually includes the line conductor and the external supply path too."
    },
    {
      "term": "Ze",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the fault path outside of the installation, provided by the electricity supplier.",
      "anchor": "The 'External' impedance measured at the origin of the installation.",
      "misconception": "Believing the electrician can lower this value; Ze is determined by the supplier's cables and transformer."
    },
    {
      "term": "R1",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the line (phase) conductor from the consumer unit to the point of the fault.",
      "anchor": "The 'Outgoing' leg of the fault current journey.",
      "misconception": "Confusing R1 with the total circuit resistance; it only accounts for the line wire."
    },
    {
      "term": "R2",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) from the point of the fault back to the main earthing terminal.",
      "anchor": "The 'Return' leg of the fault current within the building.",
      "misconception": "Thinking R2 is the same as the main earthing conductor; R2 is specific to the individual circuit's earth wire."
    },
    {
      "term": "MET",
      "simpleDefinition": "The Main Earthing Terminal; the central point where all earthing and bonding conductors are connected.",
      "anchor": "The 'Hub' where the internal house earthing meets the supplier's external earth.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the MET is just a junction box; it is a critical safety connection point for the entire installation."
    },
    {
      "term": "Supplier Earth Return Path",
      "simpleDefinition": "The physical route (cables or the ground itself) that fault current takes to get from the installation back to the supply transformer.",
      "anchor": "The 'Road Home' for electricity to complete its circuit back to the source.",
      "misconception": "Believing electricity just 'stops' at the earth rod or ground; it must always return to the transformer to complete the loop."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2)",
    "The fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.",
    "Ze is the foundation of the loop provided by the network distributor.",
    "R1 and R2 represent the internal resistance of the specific circuit."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Electricity does not disappear into the ground; it returns to the transformer.",
    "The earth loop is a circuit, not a one-way street.",
    "You cannot calculate Zs accurately without including the line conductor (R1)."
  ]
}

      "objective": "Define the earth loop impedance path as a complete circuit.",
      "teachingPoints": [
        "Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.",
        "An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.",
        "The Earth Loop Impedance Path is the complete journey that fault current takes from the transformer, through the installation, and back again.",
        "We use the symbol Zs to represent the total resistance (impedance) of this entire loop.",
        "A low Zs value is essential because it ensures enough current flows during a fault to trip a circuit breaker or blow a fuse instantly."
      ],

        "Zs",
        "Earth Loop Impedance Path",
        "Fault Current"
      ],
      "keyIdeas": [

      ],

        "Electricity 'disappears' into the earth or an earth rod without returning to the source."
      ],

        "Ze includes the resistance of the transformer windings at the substation and the supplier's distribution cables.",

Critical rules:
- basicQuestions must contain exactly 3 items.
- Each basic question must test one taught fact or one taught distinction only.
- Each basic question must be traceable to the teachingPoints from Phase 3.
- sourceTeachingPointIds must point to the teaching point indexes that justify the question.
- Each basic question answerGuidance must contain 2-5 checkable phrases or ideas.
- Never ask the learner to supply multiple separate facts in one basic question.
- Prefer single-fact or single-term checks that support precise corrective feedback.
- If a question asks for the specific term, name, abbreviation expansion, code letter, or technical label, the first answerGuidance item must be the exact target from the lesson vocabulary.
- If a question defines something and asks for its name or type, answerGuidance must contain the exact technical term that matches that definition.
- For exact-term questions, answerGuidance may include only tight equivalents of that same target. Do not accept loose descriptive paraphrases, topic labels, or nearby concepts from other questions.
- If a question asks what one code letter stands for, answerGuidance must contain only that code-letter expansion word or a tight grammatical variant, not a component name or neighbouring concept.
- Keep each answerGuidance list local to its own question only. Do not copy valid answers from adjacent questions in the same step.
- ALWAYS keep each basic question narrow, exact, and easy to mark from one response.
- ALWAYS keep exact-term answers exact. Repeat the canonical target, not a broader topic label.
- ALWAYS prefer one precise answer target over a wider but weaker guidance list.
- The deeper question must connect named teaching points and ask why/how/what-must-be-true.
- deeperAnswerGuidance must contain 3-6 checkable ideas.
- deeperSourceTeachingPointIds must identify the teaching points that justify the deeper question.

teachChecks.title: The Concept of the Loop

basicQuestions.questionText: To which specific source must fault current always return to complete its circuit?
basicQuestions.hint: This is the component at the start of the supply chain where the electricity originates.

basicQuestions.questionText: What technical symbol is used to represent the total resistance of the entire earth loop path?
basicQuestions.hint: It is a capital Z followed by a lowercase s.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the primary safety reason for maintaining a low Zs value in a circuit?
basicQuestions.hint: Think about what needs to happen to the power if a fault occurs.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Explain why the term 'loop' is used to describe the path of fault current rather than describing the earth as a simple destination.
deeperAnswerGuidance: Electricity must return to its source to flow
The path starts and ends at the transformer
It forms a complete electrical circuit
Current does not just stop at the point of the fault
teachChecks.hint: Consider what is required for any electrical current to flow continuously.

teachChecks.title: External Path Components

basicQuestions.questionText: What abbreviation represents the external earth loop impedance provided by the supplier?
At what specific point in an electrical installation is the external impedance measured?
basicQuestions.hint: This is where the supplier's cables first enter the building.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the physical route provided by the network distributor for fault current to return to the source?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the path back home provided by the company that supplies the power.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why must an electrician measure Ze at the origin of the installation if they have no direct control over the supplier's equipment?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Ze is the starting resistance for the whole loop
It is needed to calculate the total Zs
It ensures the supplier's path is adequate before adding internal wiring
It forms the foundation of the safety calculation
teachChecks.hint: Think about how the external resistance affects the final total resistance of the circuit.

teachChecks.title: Internal Path Components

basicQuestions.questionText: What label is used for the resistance of the line conductor within an installation?
basicQuestions.hint: This is the 'outbound' part of the internal circuit.

basicQuestions.questionText: What does the abbreviation R2 represent in the context of an earth loop path?
basicQuestions.hint: This is the resistance of the earth wire for that specific circuit.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the central hub where internal earthing conductors connect to the supplier's earth?
basicQuestions.hint: It is the main terminal for all earthing connections.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does the MET act as a critical junction between the work of the electrician and the network distributor?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It bridges the internal R1/R2 path and the external Ze path
It is the connection point for all earthing and bonding
It allows internal fault current to reach the supplier return path
It is the central hub for the entire earthing system
teachChecks.hint: Focus on the MET's role as a meeting point for different parts of the loop.

teachChecks.title: The Total Path (Zs)

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the standard formula used to determine the total earth loop impedance?
basicQuestions.hint: You must add the external resistance to the internal line and earth resistances.

basicQuestions.questionText: If the Zs value of a circuit is too high, what happens to the level of fault current that can flow?
basicQuestions.hint: High resistance restricts the flow of current.

basicQuestions.questionText: Which set of regulations defines the maximum allowable disconnection times for a circuit?
basicQuestions.hint: These are the 'Wiring Regulations' used in the UK.

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why would a circuit with a very high Zs value be considered a fire or shock risk even if the fuse is the correct size?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The fault current will be too low to trip the device instantly
The protective device may not disconnect within BS 7671 time limits
Current will continue to flow through the fault
The high resistance prevents the rapid operation of the fuse or breaker
teachChecks.hint: Consider the relationship between resistance, current, and the speed of the protective device.

Vocabulary output:
{
  "terms": [
    {
      "term": "Zs",
      "simpleDefinition": "The total resistance of the entire earth loop path, including both the supplier's equipment and the internal installation wiring.",
      "anchor": "The 'Total' impedance that determines if a fuse will blow fast enough during a fault.",
      "misconception": "Thinking Zs only refers to the earth wire; it actually includes the line conductor and the external supply path too."
    },
    {
      "term": "Ze",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the fault path outside of the installation, provided by the electricity supplier.",
      "anchor": "The 'External' impedance measured at the origin of the installation.",
      "misconception": "Believing the electrician can lower this value; Ze is determined by the supplier's cables and transformer."
    },
    {
      "term": "R1",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the line (phase) conductor from the consumer unit to the point of the fault.",
      "anchor": "The 'Outgoing' leg of the fault current journey.",
      "misconception": "Confusing R1 with the total circuit resistance; it only accounts for the line wire."
    },
    {
      "term": "R2",
      "simpleDefinition": "The resistance of the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC) from the point of the fault back to the main earthing terminal.",
      "anchor": "The 'Return' leg of the fault current within the building.",
      "misconception": "Thinking R2 is the same as the main earthing conductor; R2 is specific to the individual circuit's earth wire."
    },
    {
      "term": "MET",
      "simpleDefinition": "The Main Earthing Terminal; the central point where all earthing and bonding conductors are connected.",
      "anchor": "The 'Hub' where the internal house earthing meets the supplier's external earth.",
      "misconception": "Thinking the MET is just a junction box; it is a critical safety connection point for the entire installation."
    },
    {
      "term": "Supplier Earth Return Path",
      "simpleDefinition": "The physical route (cables or the ground itself) that fault current takes to get from the installation back to the supply transformer.",
      "anchor": "The 'Road Home' for electricity to complete its circuit back to the source.",
      "misconception": "Believing electricity just 'stops' at the earth rod or ground; it must always return to the transformer to complete the loop."
    }
  ],
  "anchorPhrases": [
    "Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2)",
    "The fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.",
    "Ze is the foundation of the loop provided by the network distributor.",
    "R1 and R2 represent the internal resistance of the specific circuit."
  ],
  "misconceptionTargets": [
    "Electricity does not disappear into the ground; it returns to the transformer.",
    "The earth loop is a circuit, not a one-way street.",
    "You cannot calculate Zs accurately without including the line conductor (R1)."
  ]
}

  "objective": "Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.",
    "Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.",
    "Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.",
    "Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.",

    "Final Result: The complete journey from the transformer, through the line conductor (R1), back through the CPC (R2), through the MET, and back to the transformer (Ze) forms the Earth Loop Impedance Path. The total resistance of this path is Zs.",
    "Takeaway: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal components: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2). All parts must be intact for the circuit breaker to trip safely."
  ],
  "hint": "Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier."

parsed.title: Worked Example: Tracing the Fault Path
parsed.objective: Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.

Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.

Step 4: Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.

Final Result: The complete journey from the transformer, through the line conductor (R1), back through the CPC (R2), through the MET, and back to the transformer (Ze) forms the Earth Loop Impedance Path. The total resistance of this path is Zs.
Takeaway: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal components: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2). All parts must be intact for the circuit breaker to trip safely.
parsed.hint: Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier.

Worked example output:
{
  "title": "Worked Example: Tracing the Fault Path",
  "objective": "Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).",
  "retrievalTextLines": [
    "Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.",
    "Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.",
    "Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.",
    "Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.",
    "Step 4: Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.",
    "Final Result: The complete journey from the transformer, through the line conductor (R1), back through the CPC (R2), through the MET, and back to the transformer (Ze) forms the Earth Loop Impedance Path. The total resistance of this path is Zs.",
    "Takeaway: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal components: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2). All parts must be intact for the circuit breaker to trip safely."
  ],
  "hint": "Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier."
}

Critical rules:
- guidedPractice retrieval and task must clearly mirror the worked example pattern.
- practice must be more independent than guidedPractice.
- Do not use these phases to smuggle in extra untaught chunk content that should have been its own teach/check section.
- Each phase must contain one coherent task with one response target type.
- Do not bundle several unrelated sub-questions, several scenarios, or mixed outputs into a single response field.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in one response box. If synthesis is needed, rewrite as one coherent explanation task.
- Do not ask for long ordered lists, “identify all of these”, or “answer 1, 2, 3” style tasks in these phases.
- If the learner must compare two items, the question must still produce one coherent explanation, not two separate hidden marking jobs.
- If a task currently asks for one conversion plus one classification, rewrite it so the learner gives one combined markable answer string or reduce it to one single target.
- answerGuidance must map directly to the exact response expected from that one task.
- If a task asks for a specific technical term or named item, answerGuidance must prioritise the exact target only.
- answerGuidance arrays must be concrete and gradeable.
- ALWAYS return learner-facing content, not meta-instructions such as use the same method or answer independently.
- ALWAYS replace vague placeholders with a concrete scenario, a concrete task, and concrete answer targets.
- ALWAYS keep answerGuidance as short marking points or exact targets, not long multi-clause sentences.
- Never return empty questionText, empty retrievalTextLines, or empty answerGuidance for guidedPractice or practice.
- If you are unsure, generate a narrower single-target task instead of leaving any field blank.

guidedPractice.title: Guided Practice
guidedPractice.objective: Identify the internal outbound component of the earth loop impedance path.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: A fault occurs at a metal electric heater.
The fault current starts its journey at the consumer unit and travels to the heater through the brown line conductor.
This represents the 'outgoing' leg of the internal circuit resistance.
guidedPractice.questionText: What is the technical abbreviation for the resistance of this line conductor within the earth loop path?
guidedPractice.hint: Think of the internal components: R1 is the line conductor and R2 is the CPC.

practice.title: Practice
practice.objective: Identify the total earth loop impedance abbreviation.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are completing an Electrical Installation Certificate.
You have measured the external resistance (Ze) and added it to the combined resistance of the line conductor and CPC (R1 + R2).
This value represents the resistance of the entire loop from the transformer, through the circuit, and back.
practice.questionText: What is the specific technical abbreviation for the total earth loop impedance?
practice.hint: This is the 'Total' value calculated by the formula: Ze + (R1 + R2).

- Make the question synthesize the lesson ideas.
- Use one new applied scenario, not the exact worked-example scenario repeated.
- Prefer one integrative task with one coherent response target, not a loose list of mini-questions.
- Do not combine factual recall, list-making, and open explanation in the same response field.
- Do not ask for four or five separate identifications in one box.
- Do not write identify-plus-explain bundles in the integrative phase. Ask for one applied explanation that naturally includes the required relationship.
- If a draft integrative idea asks for both a system type and a component name, rewrite it as one explanation of why that component is correct for that system scenario.
- Do not introduce a new dimension, such as safety, cost, or installation method, unless that dimension was explicitly taught in the relevant chunk.
- If the integrative task needs comparison, keep it to one clear comparison with explicit answer targets.
- answerGuidance must contain the main points expected in a strong response and must map cleanly to the exact task asked.
- ALWAYS keep the integrative task concrete, singular, and directly markable.
- ALWAYS break expected answers into short discrete points rather than one long sentence.
- ALWAYS avoid hidden multi-part marking jobs inside one response box.

Good integrative example:
{"questionText":"Using one short explanation, explain why long-distance transmission uses very high voltage.","answerGuidance":["lower current","less heat loss","higher efficiency"]}
Bad integrative example:
{"questionText":"Explain generation, transmission, voltages, and safety.","answerGuidance":["High voltage is used because it reduces current and heat loss and is efficient and connects to the National Grid and keeps the system safe over long distances."]}
Good comparison integrative example:
{"questionText":"Using one short explanation, explain why a cable with higher resistance gives a bigger voltage drop when the same current flows.","answerGuidance":["same current","higher resistance","bigger voltage drop"]}
Bad comparison integrative example:
{"questionText":"Compare resistance, resistivity, voltage drop, current, and safety.","answerGuidance":["Resistance and resistivity are different because resistivity is a material property and resistance is total opposition and this changes voltage drop and affects current and safe operation."]}

Completion requirement:
- Always return one concrete integrative task.
- Always keep integrative answer guidance as short discrete marking points.

integrative.title: The Complete Fault Journey
integrative.objective: Synthesize the external and internal components of the earth loop impedance path into a single explanation of the fault current journey.
retrievalTextLines: Fault current must complete a full loop back to the supply transformer.
The total impedance (Zs) consists of both external (Ze) and internal (R1 + R2) resistances.
The MET serves as the connection point between these two sections.
integrative.questionText: A fault occurs on a final circuit. Explain how the total earth loop impedance (Zs) is formed by describing the journey of the fault current through the specific external and internal components of the system.
answerGuidance: Ze represents the external path from the transformer to the installation origin.
R1 is the resistance of the line conductor carrying current to the point of the fault.
R2 is the resistance of the CPC carrying current from the fault back to the MET.
Zs is the total sum of these components: Ze + (R1 + R2).
integrative.hint: Trace the current from the transformer, through the line wire, through the earth wire, and back to the supplier's return path.

steps.title: The Concept of the Loop
steps.objective: Define the earth loop impedance path as a complete circuit.
keyIdeas: Fault current requires a complete loop to travel back to the transformer.
The total resistance of this loop (Zs) determines if protective devices will operate correctly.
The fault current path from the source, through the installation, and back to the source.
Fault current must have a low-resistance path to ensure protective devices operate correctly.
Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.
anchorFacts: Electricity does not simply disappear into the ground; it must always return to its source, which is the supply transformer.
An earth fault occurs when a live conductor touches metalwork, creating a circuit that allows current to flow back to the source.
The Earth Loop Impedance Path is the complete journey that fault current takes from the transformer, through the installation, and back again.
We use the symbol Zs to represent the total resistance (impedance) of this entire loop.
A low Zs value is essential because it ensures enough current flows during a fault to trip a circuit breaker or blow a fuse instantly.
Fault current must travel in a complete loop to trigger a protective device.

steps.title: The Total Path (Zs)
steps.objective: Explain the relationship between all components in the path.
keyIdeas: Zs is the total resistance of the entire journey from the source and back.
The relationship between Ze, R1, and R2 determines the overall safety of the circuit.
The formula Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2) as the total earth loop impedance.
The total impedance (Zs) determines if the circuit is safe and meets BS 7671 requirements.
The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external path and the internal path.
The standard formula used by electricians is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
anchorFacts: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external path and the internal path.
The standard formula used by electricians is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).
By adding the external resistance (Ze) to the internal cable resistances (R1 + R2), we find the total resistance a fault current will face.
This total value must be low enough to allow a high 'fault current' to flow, which forces the fuse or breaker to disconnect the power within the time limits set by BS 7671.
If the Zs value is too high, the circuit is considered unsafe because the protective device might not trip during a fault.
The formula for total earth loop impedance is Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2).

Current will continue to flow through the fault
The high resistance prevents the rapid operation of the fuse or breaker
steps.hint: To get the total (Zs), you must add the external part (Ze) to the internal parts (R1 and R2).

steps.title: Worked Example: Tracing the Fault Path
steps.objective: Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).
keyIdeas: Trace the path of a fault current from a phase failure at a socket outlet back to the supply transformer, labeling each component (R1, R2, MET, Ze).
Show one worked example.
Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.

Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.
anchorFacts: Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.

Step 1: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Step 2: Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.

Step 4: Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.
taskConstraints: Use only the terms defined in the source text.
Ensure the distinction between R1 and R2 is clear before introducing Zs.
Focus on identification of components rather than mathematical calculation.
Do not drift into: Detailed differences between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems
Do not drift into: Main protective bonding and supplementary bonding

taskSkeleton.scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.
steps: Identify the outbound path (R1). The fault current starts its journey from the consumer unit, traveling through the line conductor to the point of the fault at the socket. We label this internal resistance R1.

Identify the internal return path (R2). Once the current hits the metal back-box, it enters the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC). It travels back through the CPC to the consumer unit. We label this internal resistance R2.

Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.

Identify the external path (Ze). From the MET, the current leaves the building and travels through the supplier earth return path (such as the cable armouring) all the way back to the supply transformer. This external resistance is labeled Ze.
taskSkeleton.takeaway: The total earth loop impedance (Zs) is the sum of the external and internal components: Zs = Ze + (R1 + R2). All parts must be intact for the circuit breaker to trip safely.

steps.hint: Remember that Zs is the 'Total' loop, while Ze is just the 'External' part provided by the supplier.

spacedReview.title: Spaced Review: Earthing System Fundamentals
spacedReview.objective: Recall the three main earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) to provide context for the earth loop impedance path.
retrievalTextLines: Before we map the fault path, remember the three main earthing systems: TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S.
In a TN-S system, the supplier provides a separate earth conductor, often using the cable's lead sheath.
In a TN-C-S system (PME), the neutral and earth are combined in the supplier's network and split at the intake.
In a TT system, there is no supplier earth provided; the installation relies on the mass of the earth via an electrode.
spacedReview.questionText: Which earthing system uses a combined neutral and earth conductor in the supplier's network before splitting them into separate terminals at the consumer's origin?
answerGuidance: N = Neutral (Connection to the transformer neutral).

        "Scenario: You are investigating a fault at a single socket outlet where the line conductor has come loose and is touching the metal back-box.",

        "Step 3: Identify the connection point (MET). The CPC connects to the earthing bar, which is linked to the Main Earthing Terminal (MET). This is the central hub where the internal wiring meets the supplier's equipment.",
